| Bird | [Himalayan monal](/wiki/Himalayan_monal "Himalayan monal") |
| Flower | [Rhododendron arboreum](/wiki/Rhododendron_arboreum "Rhododendron arboreum")[[315]](#cite_note-327) |
| Mammal | [Cow](/wiki/Cow "Cow")[[314]](#cite_note-326) |
| Colour | [Crimson](/wiki/Crimson "Crimson") |
| Sport | [Volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball")[[316]](#cite_note-voll-328) |

The emblem of Nepal depicts the snowy Himalayas, the forested hills, and the fertile Terai, supported by a wreath of rhododendrons, with the national flag at the crest and in the foreground, a plain white map of Nepal below it, and a man's and woman's right hands joined to signify gender equality. At the bottom is the national motto, a Sanskrit quote of patriotism attributed in Nepali folklore to Lord [Rama](/wiki/Rama "Rama"), written in Devanagari script—"*Mother and motherland are greater than heaven*".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Nepal's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Nepal "Flag of Nepal") is the only national flag in the world that is not rectangular in shape.[[317]](#cite_note-329) The constitution contains instructions for a [Geometric Construction](/wiki/Geometric_Construction "Geometric Construction") of the double-pennant flag.[[318]](#cite_note-330) According to its official description, the crimson in the flag stands for victory in war or courage, and is also the colour of the [rhododendron](/wiki/Rhododendron "Rhododendron"). The flag's blue border signifies Nepali people's desire for peace. The moon on the flag is a symbol of the peaceful and calm nature of Nepalis, while the sun represents the aggressiveness of Nepali warriors.

The president is the symbol of national unity. The [martyrs](/wiki/Martyrs_of_Nepal "Martyrs of Nepal") are the symbols of patriotism. Commanders of the Anglo-Nepalese war, [Amar Singh Thapa](/wiki/Amar_Singh_Thapa "Amar Singh Thapa"), [Bhakti Thapa](/wiki/Bhakti_Thapa "Bhakti Thapa"), and Balbhadra Kunwar are considered war heroes. A special designation of "National hero" has been conferred to 16 people from Nepal's history for their exceptional contributions to the prestige of Nepal. Prithvi Narayan Shah, the founder of modern Nepal, is held in high regard and considered "Father of the Nation" by many.[[319]](#cite_note-331)[[320]](#cite_note-Hutt1991-332)

### Art and architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Nepal](/wiki/Architecture_of_Nepal "Architecture of Nepal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Nyatapola_Temple.JPG/250px-Nyatapola_Temple.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nyatapola_Temple.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Tusha_Hiti_step-well.jpg/250px-Tusha_Hiti_step-well.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tusha_Hiti_step-well.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Shivas_Kinder_-_0219.jpg/330px-Shivas_Kinder_-_0219.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shivas_Kinder_-_0219.jpg)

Clockwise from top-left: (a) [Nyatapola](/wiki/Nyatapola "Nyatapola"), a five storied [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda") in Bhaktapur, bejewelled with characteristic stone, metal and wood craftsmanship, has survived at least four major earthquakes.[[321]](#cite_note-unesco-333) Pagodas, now an indispensable part of East Asian architecture, are conjectured to have been transmitted to China from Nepal. (b) Nepali stonecraft in a royal water spout. (c) A traditional Newar "Ankhijhyal" window in the form of a peacock.

The oldest known examples of architecture in Nepal are [stupas](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") of early Buddhist constructions in and around [Kapilvastu](/wiki/Kapilvastu_District "Kapilvastu District") in south-western Nepal, and those constructed by [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") in the [Kathmandu Valley](/wiki/Kathmandu_Valley "Kathmandu Valley") c. 250 BC. The characteristic architecture associated exclusively with Nepal was developed and refined by Newa artisans of the Kathmandu Valley starting no later than the Lichchhavi period. A [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") Chinese travel book, probably based on records from c. 650 AD, describes contemporary Nepali architecture, predominantly built with wood, as rich in artistry, as well as wood and metal sculpture. It describes a magnificent seven-storied pagoda in the middle of a palace, with copper-tiled roofs, its balustrade, grills, columns and beams set about with fine and precious stones, and four golden sculptures of [Makaras](/wiki/Makara "Makara") in the four corners of the base spouting water from their mouths like a fountain, supplied by copper pipes connected to the runnels at the top of the tower. Later Chinese chronicles describe Nepal's king's palace as an immense structure with many roofs, suggesting that the Chinese were not yet familiar with the pagoda architecture, which has now become one of the chief characteristics of [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture").

A typical pagoda temple is built with wood, every piece of it finely carved with geometrical patterns or images of gods, goddesses, mythical beings and beasts. The roofs usually tiled with clay, and sometimes gold plated, diminish in proportion successively until the topmost roof is reached which is itself ensigned by a golden finial. The base is usually composed of rectangular terraces of finely carved stone; the entrance is usually guarded by stone sculptures of conventional figures. Bronze and copper craftsmanship observable in the sculpture of deities and beasts, decorations of doors and windows and the finials of buildings, as well as items of everyday use is found to be of equal splendour. The most well-developed of Nepali painting traditions is the [thanka](/wiki/Thanka "Thanka") or [paubha](/wiki/Paubha "Paubha") painting tradition of [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism"), practised in Nepal by the Buddhist monks and Newar artisans. [Changu Narayan Temple](/wiki/Changu_Narayan_Temple "Changu Narayan Temple"), built c. 4th century AD has probably the finest of Nepali woodcraft; the Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur Durbar Squares are the culmination of Nepali art and architecture, showcasing Nepali wood, metal and stone craftsmanship refined over two millennia.[[322]](#cite_note-landon2-334)

The ["ankhijhyal" window](/wiki/Newar_window "Newar window"), that allow a one-way view of the outside world, is an example of unique Nepali woodcraft, found in building structures, domestic and public alike, ancient and modern. Many cultures paint the walls of their homes with regular patterns, figures of gods and beasts and religious symbols; others paint their walls plain, often with clay or [chernozem](/wiki/Chernozem "Chernozem") contrasted with yellow soil or limestone. The roofs of religious as well as domestic structures project considerably, presumably to provide protection from the sun and the rain. The timber of domestic structures are finely carved as with their religious counterparts.[[322]](#cite_note-landon2-334)

### Looting of the cultural heritage of Nepal

Cultural looting is a crisis in Nepal despite efforts to combat it.[[323]](#cite_note-335)[[324]](#cite_note-336)[[325]](#cite_note-337) Volunteers working for the Nepal Heritage Recovery Campaign have helped recover artifacts.[[326]](#cite_note-338) In 2022 [Barakat Gallery](/wiki/Barakat_Gallery "Barakat Gallery")'s London branch relinquished 16th-century carved wooden Torana, a ceremonial gateway, and the 17th-century stone statue of a kneeling devotee, both taken from sacred sites near Kathmandu.[[327]](#cite_note-339) In 2023 Nepalese artifacts suspected of having been looted were found in the [Art Institute of Chicago](/wiki/Art_Institute_of_Chicago "Art Institute of Chicago"),[[328]](#cite_note-340) the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"),[[329]](#cite_note-341)[[330]](#cite_note-342) the [Dallas Museum of Art](/wiki/Dallas_Museum_of_Art "Dallas Museum of Art"),[[331]](#cite_note-343) the [Rubin Museum](/wiki/Rubin_Museum_of_Art "Rubin Museum of Art")[[332]](#cite_note-344) and other museums, and turned up in auctions at [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's"),[[333]](#cite_note-345) [Bonhams](/wiki/Bonhams "Bonhams")[[334]](#cite_note-346) and other auction houses.

### Literature and the performing arts

Main articles: [Nepalese literature](/wiki/Nepalese_literature "Nepalese literature"), [Music of Nepal](/wiki/Music_of_Nepal "Music of Nepal"), and [Cinema of Nepal](/wiki/Cinema_of_Nepal "Cinema of Nepal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Bhanu_Bhakta_Acharya_%281814-1869%29_author.jpg/250px-Bhanu_Bhakta_Acharya_%281814-1869%29_author.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bhanu_Bhakta_Acharya_(1814-1869)_author.jpg)

[Bhanubhakta Acharya](/wiki/Bhanubhakta_Acharya "Bhanubhakta Acharya"), Nepali writer who translated the ancient Hindu epic *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")* in the [Nepali language](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language")

Nepal's literature was closely intertwined with that of the rest of South Asia until its unification into a modern kingdom. Literary works, which were written in Sanskrit by [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") priests educated and sometimes also based in [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi"), included religious texts and legends involving kings, gods and demons.[[335]](#cite_note-bba-347) The oldest extant Nepali language text is dated to the 13th century but except for the epigraphic material, Nepali language literature older than the 17th century have not been found. [Newar literature](/wiki/Newar_literature "Newar literature") dates back almost 500 years.[[320]](#cite_note-Hutt1991-332)

The modern history of [Nepali literature](/wiki/Nepali_literature "Nepali literature") begins with Bhanubhakta Acharya (1814–1868), who for the first time composed major and influential works in Nepali, the language accessible to the masses, most prominently, the [Bhanubhakta Ramayana](/wiki/Bhanubhakta_Ramayana "Bhanubhakta Ramayana"), a translation of the ancient Hindu epic.[[335]](#cite_note-bba-347) By the end of the nineteenth century, [Motiram Bhatta](/wiki/Motiram_Bhatta "Motiram Bhatta") had published print editions of the works of Acharya, and through his efforts, single-handedly popularised and propelled Nepali language literature into modernity.[[320]](#cite_note-Hutt1991-332) By the mid-twentieth century, Nepali literature was no longer limited to the Hindu literary traditions. Influenced by western literary traditions, writers in this period started producing literary works addressing the contemporary social problems,[[336]](#cite_note-348) while many others continued to enrich Nepali poetic traditions with authentic Nepali poetry. Newar literature also emerged as a premier literary tradition. After the advent of democracy in 1951, Nepali literature flourished. Literary works in many other languages began to be produced. Nepali literature continued to modernise, and in recent years, has been strongly influenced by the post civil-war Nepali experience as well as global literary traditions.[[337]](#cite_note-349)[[338]](#cite_note-350)[[339]](#cite_note-351)[[320]](#cite_note-Hutt1991-332)

[Maruni](/wiki/Maruni "Maruni"), [Lakhey](/wiki/Lakhey "Lakhey"), [Sakela](/wiki/Sakela "Sakela"), [Kauda](/wiki/Kauda "Kauda") and [Tamang Selo](/wiki/Tamang_Selo "Tamang Selo") are some examples of the traditional Nepali music and dance in the hilly regions of Nepal.

The Nepali film industry is known as ["Kollywood"](/wiki/Kollywood_(Kathmandu) "Kollywood (Kathmandu)").[[340]](#cite_note-352)

[Nepal Academy](/wiki/Nepal_Academy "Nepal Academy") is the foremost institution for the promotion of arts and culture in Nepal, established in 1957.[[320]](#cite_note-Hutt1991-332)

### Clothing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Bhoto_jatra.jpg/250px-Bhoto_jatra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bhoto_jatra.jpg)

A Nepali man in Daura-Suruwal, coat and Dhaka topi, displays the bhoto during the [Bhoto Jatra](/wiki/Bhoto_Jatra "Bhoto Jatra") festival.

The most widely worn traditional dress in Nepal, for both women and men, from ancient times until the advent of modern times, was draped.[[341]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-353) For women, it eventually took the form of a [sari](/wiki/Sari "Sari"), a single long piece of cloth, famously six yards long, and of width spanning the lower body.[[341]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-353) The sari is tied around the waist and knotted at one end, wrapped around the lower body, and then over the shoulder.[[341]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-353) In its more modern form, it has been used to cover the head, and sometimes the face, as a veil,[[341]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-353) particularly in the Terai. It has been combined with an underskirt, or the [petticoat](/wiki/Petticoat#Asian_petticoats "Petticoat"), and tucked in the waistband for more secure fastening. It is worn with a [blouse](/wiki/Blouse "Blouse"), or [cholo](/wiki/Choli "Choli"), which serves as the primary upper-body garment, the sari's end, passing over the shoulder, now serving to obscure the upper body's contours, and to cover the midriff.[[341]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-353) Cholo-sari has become the attire of choice for formal occasions, official environs and festive gatherings. In its more traditional form, as part of traditional dresses and as worn in daily life while performing household chores or labour, it takes the form of a *fariya* or *gunyu*, usually shorter than a sari in length as well as breadth, and all of it wrapped around the lower body.

For men, a similar but shorter length of cloth, the [dhoti](/wiki/Dhoti "Dhoti"), has served as a lower-body garment.[[342]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-28-354) It too is tied around the waist and wrapped.[[342]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-28-354) Among the Aryans, it is also wrapped once around each leg before being brought up through the legs to be tucked in at the back. Dhoti or its variants, usually worn over a [langauti](/wiki/Kaupinam "Kaupinam"), constitute the lower-body garment in the traditional clothing of Tharus, Gurungs and Magars as well as the [Madheshi people](/wiki/Madheshi_people "Madheshi people"), among others. Other forms of traditional apparel that involve no stitching or tailoring are [patukas](/wiki/Patuka "Patuka") (a length of cloth wrapped tightly over the waist by both sexes as a waistband, a part of most traditional Nepali costumes, usually with a khukuri tucked into it when worn by men), scarves like *pachhyauras* and *majetros* and shawls like the newar *ga* and Tibetan [khata](/wiki/Khata "Khata"), ghumtos (the wedding veils) and various kinds of [turbans](/wiki/Turban "Turban") (scarves worn around the head as a part of a tradition, or to keep off the sun or the cold,[[342]](#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-28-354) called a [pheta](/wiki/Pheta "Pheta"), pagri or sirpau).

Until the beginning of the first millennium AD, the ordinary dress of people in South Asia was entirely unstitched.[[343]](#cite_note-Rahman-Alkazi2002-355) The arrival of the Kushans from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), c. 48 AD, popularised cut and sewn garments in the style of Central Asia.[[343]](#cite_note-Rahman-Alkazi2002-355) The simplest form of sewn clothing, Bhoto (a rudimentary vest), is a universal unisex clothing for children, and traditionally the only clothing children wear until they come of age and are given adult garb, sometimes in a ceremonial rite of passage, such as the gunyu-choli ceremony for Hindu girls. Men continue to wear bhoto through adulthood. Upper body garment for men is usually a vest such as the *bhoto*, or a shirt similar to the *[kurta](/wiki/Kurta "Kurta")*, such as *daura*, a closed-necked double-breasted long shirt with five pleats and eight strings that serve to tie it around the body. *Suruwal*, simply translated as a pair of trousers, is an alternative to and, more recently, replacement for *dhoti*, *kachhad* (Magars) or *lungi* (Tharus); it is traditionally much wider above the knees but tapers below, to fit tightly at the ankles, and is tied to the waist with a drawstring. Modern cholos worn with sarees are usually half-sleeved and single-breasted, and do not cover the midriff. The traditional one called the chaubandi cholo, like the daura, is full-sleeved, double-breasted with pleats and strings, and extends down to the patuka, covering the midriff.

[Daura-Suruwal](/wiki/Daura-Suruwal "Daura-Suruwal") and Gunyu-Cholo were the national dresses for men and women respectively until 2011 when they were removed to eliminate favouritism.[[344]](#cite_note-356) Traditional dresses of many pahari ethnic groups are Daura-Suruwal or similar, with patuka, a dhaka topi and a coat for men, and Gunyu-cholo or similar, with patuka and sometimes a scarf for women. For many other groups, men's traditional dresses consist of a shirt or a vest, paired with a *dhoti*, *kachhad* or *lungi*. In the high Himalayas, the traditional dresses are largely influenced by [Tibetan culture](/wiki/Tibetan_culture "Tibetan culture"). Sherpa women wear the [chuba](/wiki/Chuba "Chuba") with the [pangi](/w/index.php?title=Pangi_(apron)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pangi (apron) (page does not exist)") apron, while Sherpa men wear shirts with stiff high collar and long sleeves called tetung under the chuba. Tibetan Xamo Gyaise hats of the Sherpas, [dhaka topi](/wiki/Dhaka_topi "Dhaka topi") of pahari men and tamang round caps are among the more distinctive headwears.

Married Hindu women wear *tika*, *sindur*, *pote* and red bangles. Jewellery of gold and silver, and sometimes precious stones, are common. Gold jewellery includes *mangalsutras* and *tilaharis* worn with the *pote* by the Hindus, *samyafung* (a huge gold flower worn on the head) and Nessey (huge flattened gold earrings) worn by the Limbus, and *sirphuli*, *sirbandhi* and *chandra* worn by the Magars. Tharu women can wear as much as six kilograms of silver in jewellery, which includes *mangiya* worn on the head, *tikuli* the forehead, and *kanseri* and *tikahamala* around the neck.[[345]](#cite_note-357)

In the last 50 years, fashions have changed a great deal in Nepal. Increasingly, in urban settings, the sari is no longer the apparel of everyday wear, transformed instead into one for formal occasions. The traditional kurta suruwal is rarely worn by younger women, who increasingly favour [jeans](/wiki/Jeans "Jeans"). The *dhoti* has largely been reduced to the [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgical "Liturgical") [vestment](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") of shamans and Hindu priests.

### Cuisine

Main article: [Nepalese cuisine](/wiki/Nepalese_cuisine "Nepalese cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Nepali_dal-bhat-tarkari.jpg/250px-Nepali_dal-bhat-tarkari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nepali_dal-bhat-tarkari.jpg)

A dal-bhat thali with boiled rice, lentil soup, fried leafy greens, vegetable curry, yoghurt, papad and vegetable salad

Nepali cuisine consists of a wide variety of regional and traditional cuisines. With diversity in soil type, climate, culture, ethnic groups, and occupations, these cuisines vary substantially, using locally available spices, herbs, vegetables, and fruit.[[346]](#cite_note-tkpcrop-358) The [Columbian exchange](/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange") brought potatoes, tomatoes, maize, peanuts, cashew nuts, pineapples, guavas, and most notably, [chilli peppers](/wiki/Chilli_peppers "Chilli peppers"), to South Asia; all became staples.[[347]](#cite_note-359) [Cereals](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal") grown in Nepal, their times and regions of planting, correspond strongly to the timing of monsoons[[348]](#cite_note-sen-colleen-p13-360) and variations in altitude. Rice and wheat are mostly cultivated in the terai plains and well-irrigated valleys; maize, millet, barley and buckwheat mostly in the less fertile and drier hills.[[346]](#cite_note-tkpcrop-358)[[349]](#cite_note-pathak-361)

A typical Nepali meal is a cereal cooked in plain fashion, complemented with flavourful, savoury dishes.[[350]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-p409-362) The latter include [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), [pulses](/wiki/Pulses "Pulses") and vegetables, spiced commonly with [ginger](/wiki/Ginger_root "Ginger root") and [garlic](/wiki/Garlic "Garlic"), and more discerningly with combinations of [coriander](/wiki/Coriander "Coriander"), [cumin](/wiki/Cumin "Cumin"), [turmeric](/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric"), [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon"), [cardamon](/wiki/Cardamon "Cardamon"), [jimbu](/wiki/Jimbu "Jimbu") and others.[[350]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-p409-362) This is typically on a platter, or [thali](/wiki/Thali "Thali"), with a central place for the cooked cereal and small bowls for the flavourful accompaniments. They are combined either by actual mixing—for example, rice and lentils—or in the folding of one—such as bread—around the other, such as cooked vegetables.[[350]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-p409-362) *Dal-bhat* centred around steamed rice is the most common example.[[351]](#cite_note-cnnfood-363) as well as dairy and sometimes meat. Unleavened flat bread made from wheat flour called [chapati](/wiki/Chapati "Chapati") occasionally replaces rice, particularly in the Terai, while [Dhindo](/wiki/Dhindo "Dhindo"), prepared by boiling corn, millet or buckwheat flour in water, continuously stirring and adding flour until thick, almost solid consistency is reached, is the main substitute in the hills and mountains. Tsampa, flour made from roasted barley or naked barley, is the main staple in the high himalayas. Throughout Nepal, fermented, then sun-dried, leafy greens called *Gundruk*, are both a delicacy and a vital substitute for fresh vegetables in the winter.[[349]](#cite_note-pathak-361)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Plateful_of_Momo_in_Nepal.jpg/250px-Plateful_of_Momo_in_Nepal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plateful_of_Momo_in_Nepal.jpg)

Momo dumplings with chutney

A notable feature of Nepali food is the existence of a number of distinctive vegetarian cuisines, each a feature of the geographical and cultural histories of its adherents.[[352]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-p410-364) The appearance of *[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa")*, or the avoidance of violence toward all forms of life in many religious orders early in South Asian history, especially [Upanishadic Hinduism](/wiki/Upanishads "Upanishads"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), is thought to have been a notable factor in the prevalence of vegetarianism among a segment of Nepal's Hindu and Buddhist populations, as well as among Jains.[[352]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-p410-364) Among these groups, strong discomfort is felt at thoughts of eating meat.[[353]](#cite_note-SahakianSaloma2016-50-365) Though per capita meat consumption is low in Nepal, the proportion of vegetarianism is not high as in India, due to the prevalence of [Shaktism](/wiki/Shaktism "Shaktism"), of which animal sacrifice is a prominent feature.[[354]](#cite_note-366)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Samaybaji.JPG/250px-Samaybaji.JPG)](/wiki/File:Samaybaji.JPG)

Samayabaji (Newar cuisine)

Nepali cuisines possess their own distinctive qualities to distinguish these hybrid cuisines from both their northern and southern neighbours.[[355]](#cite_note-367)[[351]](#cite_note-cnnfood-363) Nepali cuisines, with generally tomato-based, leaner curries, are lighter than their cream-based Indian counterparts, and Nepali momo dumplings are heavily spiced compared to their northern counterparts.[[351]](#cite_note-cnnfood-363) Newar cuisine, one of the richest and most influential in Nepal, is more elaborate and diverse than most, as Newar culture developed in the highly fertile and prosperous Kathmandu valley.[[346]](#cite_note-tkpcrop-358) A typical Newar cuisine can comprise more than a dozen dishes of cereals, meat, vegetable curries, chutneys and pickles. [Kwanti](/wiki/Kwati_(soup) "Kwati (soup)") (sprouted beans soup), chhwela (ground beef), [chatamari](/w/index.php?title=Chat%C4%81nmari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chatānmari (page does not exist)") (rice flour crepe), bara (fried lentil cake), kachila (marinated raw minced beef), samaybaji (centred around flattened rice), *lakhaamari* and *yomuri* are among the more widely recognised.[[351]](#cite_note-cnnfood-363)[[356]](#cite_note-368) Juju dhau, a sweet yoghurt originating in Bhaktapur, is also famous.[[351]](#cite_note-cnnfood-363) Thakali cuisine is another well-known food tradition which seamlessly melds the Tibetan and the Indian with variety in ingredients, especially the herbs and spices.[[346]](#cite_note-tkpcrop-358) In the Terai, [Bagiya](/wiki/Bagiya "Bagiya") is a rice flour dumpling with sweets inside, popular among the Tharu and Maithil people. Various communities in the Terai make *sidhara* (sun-dried small fish mixed with [taro](/wiki/Taro "Taro") leaves) and biriya (lentil paste mixed with taro leaves) to stock for the monsoon floods.[[346]](#cite_note-tkpcrop-358) *Selroti*, *kasaar*, *fini* and *chaku* are among the sweet delicacies. Rice pulau or sweet rice porridge called *kheer* are usually the main dish in feasts.[[349]](#cite_note-pathak-361) Tea and buttermilk (fermented milk leftover from churning butter from yoghurt) are common non-alcoholic drinks. Almost all janajati communities have their own traditional methods of brewing alcohol. [Raksi](/wiki/Raksi "Raksi") (traditional distilled alcohol), jaand (rice beer), tongba (millet beer) and chyaang are the most well-known.

### Sports and recreation

Main article: [Sports in Nepal](/wiki/Sports_in_Nepal "Sports in Nepal")

[Nepali indigenous sports](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_Nepal "Traditional games of Nepal"), like [dandi biyo](/wiki/Dandi_biyo "Dandi biyo") and [kabaddi](/wiki/Kabaddi "Kabaddi") which were considered the unofficial national sports until recently,[[316]](#cite_note-voll-328) are still popular in rural areas.[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) Despite efforts, standardisation and development of dandi biyo has not been achieved,[[358]](#cite_note-Online_Khabar_national_sport-370)[[359]](#cite_note-371) while Kabaddi, as a professional sport, is still in its infancy in Nepal.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Bagh-chal](/wiki/Bagh-chal "Bagh-chal"), an ancient [board game](/wiki/Board_game "Board game") that is thought to have originated in Nepal, can be played on chalk-drawn boards, with pebbles, and is still popular today.[[360]](#cite_note-372)[[361]](#cite_note-373) [Ludo](/wiki/Ludo_(board_game) "Ludo (board game)"), [snakes and ladders](/wiki/Snakes_and_ladders "Snakes and ladders") and [carrom](/wiki/Carrom "Carrom") are popular pastimes.[[362]](#cite_note-374) [Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") is also played.[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) [Volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") was declared as the national sport of Nepal in 2017.[[316]](#cite_note-voll-328) Popular children's games include versions of [tag](/wiki/Tag_(game) "Tag (game)"),[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) [knucklebones](/wiki/Knucklebones "Knucklebones"),[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) [hopscotch](/wiki/Hopscotch "Hopscotch"), [Duck, duck, goose](/wiki/Duck,_duck,_goose#Rumaal_Chor "Duck, duck, goose")[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) and [lagori](/wiki/Seven_stones "Seven stones"), while [marbles](/wiki/Marbles "Marbles"),[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) [top](/wiki/Spinning_top "Spinning top"), [hoop rolling](/wiki/Hoop_rolling "Hoop rolling") and [gully cricket](/wiki/Gully_cricket "Gully cricket") are also popular among boys. [Rubber bands](/wiki/Rubber_band "Rubber band"), or [ranger bands](/wiki/Ranger_band "Ranger band") cut from tubes in [bike](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle") tyres, make a multi-purpose sporting equipment for Nepali children, which may be bunched or chained together, and used to play [dodgeball](/wiki/Dodgeball "Dodgeball"), [cat's cradle](/wiki/Cat%27s_cradle "Cat's cradle"), [jianzi](/wiki/Jianzi "Jianzi")[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) and a variety of [skipping rope](/wiki/Skipping_rope "Skipping rope") games.[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Nepali_Fans.JPG/250px-Nepali_Fans.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nepali_Fans.JPG)

Nepali cricket fans are renowned for their exceptionally enthusiastic support of their national team.[[363]](#cite_note-375)[[364]](#cite_note-376)

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") are popular professional sports.[[365]](#cite_note-377) Nepal is competitive in football in the South Asia region but has never won the [SAFF championships](/wiki/SAFF "SAFF"), but has had some success in [South Asian Games](/wiki/South_Asian_Games "South Asian Games").[[366]](#cite_note-378)[[367]](#cite_note-379) It usually ranks in the bottom quarter in the [FIFA World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_World_Rankings "FIFA World Rankings").[[368]](#cite_note-380) Nepal has had some success in cricket and holds [ODI status](/wiki/List_of_countries_with_ODI_status "List of countries with ODI status"),[[369]](#cite_note-381)[[370]](#cite_note-382) consistently ranking in the Top 20 in the [ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") [ODI](/wiki/ICC_ODI_Championship "ICC ODI Championship") and [T20I rankings](/wiki/ICC_T20I_Championship "ICC T20I Championship").[[371]](#cite_note-383)[[372]](#cite_note-384) Nepal has had some success in [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") and [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), having won many medals at the [South Asian Games](/wiki/South_Asian_Games "South Asian Games") and some at the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games").[[373]](#cite_note-trn-385) Nepal has never won an [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") medal.[[374]](#cite_note-gauri-386) Sports like [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), volleyball, [futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), competitive bodybuilding[[374]](#cite_note-gauri-386)[[375]](#cite_note-387) and [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") are also gaining in popularity.[[357]](#cite_note-Himalayan_Times_More_than_child's_play-369) Women in football, cricket, athletics, martial arts, badminton and swimming have found some success.[[376]](#cite_note-award-388)[[374]](#cite_note-gauri-386) Nepal also fields players and national teams in several tournaments for [disabled](/wiki/Disability "Disability") individuals, most notably in men's[[377]](#cite_note-menb-389) as well as women's blind cricket.[[378]](#cite_note-wmenb-390)

The only international stadium in the country is the multi-purpose [Dasarath Stadium](/wiki/Dasharath_Rangasala "Dasharath Rangasala") where the [men](/wiki/Nepal_national_football_team "Nepal national football team") and [women national football teams](/wiki/Nepal_women%27s_national_football_team "Nepal women's national football team") play their home matches.[[379]](#cite_note-dash-391) Since the formation of the [national team](/wiki/Nepal_national_cricket_team "Nepal national cricket team"), Nepal has played its home matches of cricket at [Tribhuvan University International Cricket Ground](/wiki/Tribhuvan_University_International_Cricket_Ground "Tribhuvan University International Cricket Ground").[[380]](#cite_note-cricktarchv-392) Nepal police, Armed police force and Nepal army are the most prolific producers of national players, and aspiring players are known to join armed forces, for the better sporting opportunities they can provide.[[381]](#cite_note-393)[[382]](#cite_note-394) Nepali sports is hindered by a lack of infrastructure,[[383]](#cite_note-395) funding,[[377]](#cite_note-menb-389) corruption, nepotism and political interference.[[373]](#cite_note-trn-385)[[384]](#cite_note-396)[[385]](#cite_note-397) Very few players are able to make a living as professional sportspeople.[[379]](#cite_note-dash-391)[[386]](#cite_note-398)
